HomePage.Greeting1: Welcome to Gjallarbru — a portal inspired by ancient Scandinavian mythology and its profound bond between the human and the divine.
HomePage.Greeting2: In the myths, Gjallarbrú is the bridge that crosses the river Gjöll leading to Helheim, the realm of the dead. Yet, in a symbolic sense, it is the path of understanding that connects us with the world of spirits, the memory of our ancestors, and the gods themselves. This website is our bridge — where ancient tales, images, and archetypes come alive, passed down through millennia. Myths are the link between the past and the present, where each of us may find our own way across this bridge — toward meaning, strength, and the inspiration of the Northern tradition.

TheProphecyOfTheSeeress.Title: The Prophecy of the Seeress
TheProphecyOfTheSeeress.Source:
An ancient Icelandic text, edited by Guðni Jónsson.
Translation by Henry Adams Bellows.

And power to see the sun;

(Short are the yards of a ship,)
And more in a month's time.
Yet scorn for him none should know.
Among Fitjung's sons saw I well-stocked folds,–
Now bear they the beggar's staff;
Wealth is as swift as a winking eye,
Of friends the falsest it is.
And so one dies one's self;
But a noble name will never die,
And so one dies one's self;
One thing now that never dies,
The fame of a dead man's deeds.
Certain is that which is sought from runes,
That the gods so great have made,
An unwise man, if a maiden's love
Or wealth he chances to win,
His pride will wax, but his wisdom never,
Straight forward he fares in conceit.
Give praise to the day at evening,
to a woman on her pyre,

to a maid at wed lock,

in fair winds seek the water;
Buy a steed that is lean,

For their hearts on a whirling
And fickle their breasts were formed.

as to trust in them all.

For what touches many a man;

when I sat in the reeds,

if a man but tries them,

A man shall be wary and wise;

Of the sons of King Hrauthung

King Hrauthung had two sons: one was called Agnar, and the other Geirröth. Agnar was ten winters old, and Geirröth eight. Once they both rowed in a boat with their fishing-gear to catch little fish; and the wind drove them out into the sea. In the darkness of the night they were wrecked on the shore; and going up, they found a poor peasant, with whom they stayed through the winter. The housewife took care of Agnar, and the peasant cared for Geirröth, and taught him wisdom. In the spring the peasant gave him a boat; and when the couple led them to the shore, the peasant spoke secretly with Geirröth. They had a fair wind, and came to their father's landing-place. Geirröth was forward in the boat; he leaped up on land, but pushed out the boat and said, "Go thou now where evil may have thee!" The boat drifted out to sea. Geirröth, however, went up to the house, and was well received, but his father was dead. Then Geirröth was made king, and became a renowned man.

Othin and Frigg sat in Hlithskjolf and looked over all the worlds. Othin said: "Seest thou Agnar, thy foster ling, how he begets children with a giantess in the cave? But Geirröth, my fosterling, is a king, and now rules over his land." Frigg said: "He is so miserly that he tortures his guests if he thinks that too many of them come to him." Othin replied that this was the greatest of lies; and they made a wager about this matter. Frigg sent her maid-servant, Fulla, to Geirröth. She bade the king beware lest a magician who was come thither to his land should bewitch him, and told this sign concerning him, that no dog was so fierce as to leap at him. Now it was a very great slander that King Geirröth was not hospitable; but nevertheless he had them take the man whom the dogs would not attack. He wore a dark-blue mantle and called himself Grimnir, but said no more about himself, though he was questioned. The king had him tortured to make him speak, and set him between two fires, and he sat there eight nights. King Geirröth had a son ten winters old, and called Agnar after his father's brother. Agnar went to Grimnir, and gave him a full horn to drink from, and said that the king did ill in letting him be tormented with out cause. Grimnir drank from the horn; the fire had come so near that the mantle burned on Grimnir's back. He spake:
Get ye now gone, ye flames!
And the fire scorches the fur.
And no man brought meat to me,
The gods and the elves together;
Till the gods to destruction go.
A hall for himself hath set;
As a tooth-gift in ancient times.
By the hands of the gracious gods:
Set by a god for himself.
And amid their murmur it stands;
In gladness from cups of gold.
The men who have fallen in fight.
On its benches are breastplates strewn.
And o'er it an eagle hovers.
In the home that her father had.
And from evil fate is free.
O'er men holds sway, it is said;
Who shall have seats in the hall;
And its roof with silver is set;
And sets all strife at end.
When his father he fain would avenge.
On what fare the warriors feast.
Each day set forth to fly;
But for Munin my care is more.

And behind are the holy doors;
Who can tell how it tightly is locked.
When to war with the wolf they go.

That flow through the fields of the gods;
And hence they fall to Hel.

On these steeds the gods shall go
'Neath the last are the lands of men.
And tells them to Nithhogg beneath.
Than an unwise ape would think;
Gnaw at the twigs of the tree.
Far more than men do know;
bring the horn at my will,

Of yore set under their yokes.
The shield for the shining god;
If it fell from before the sun.
And the ocean out of his blood;
Of his skull the heavens high.
And set for the sons of men;
They made to move on high.
Who first in the flames will reach;
For the house can be seen
by the sons of the gods
If the kettle aside were cast.
And Sleipnir the best of steeds;
To the race of the gods
And the wished-for aid have I waked;
Since first among men I fared.
when I went in a sledge,
At the council Thror am I called,
As Vithur I fare to the fight;
As Svithur and Svithrir of yore;
When the famed one found his doom.
From me or my heroes thou hast.
And false were the words of thy friends;
That waits all wet with blood.
For thy life is ended at last;
Now come to me if thou canst!
Ere that did they call me Thund;
Gaut and Jalk midst the gods;
Are names for none but me.

King Geirröth sat and had his sword on his knee, half drawn from its sheath. But when he heard that Othin was come thither, then he rose up and sought to take Othin from the fire. The sword slipped from his hand, and fell with the hilt down. The king stumbled and fell forward, and the sword pierced him through, and slew him. Then Othin vanished, but Agnar long ruled there as king.

Freyr, the son of Njorth, had sat one day in Hlithskjolf, and looked over all the worlds. He looked into Jotunheim, and saw there a fair maiden, as she went from her father's house to her bower. Forthwith he felt a mighty love-sickness. Skirnir was the name of Freyr's servant; Njorth bade him ask speech of Freyr. He said:
If I seek to get speech with him,
For now I fain would know;
Of all my grief so great?
That thou mayst not tell them to me;
We two might each other trust."
Shone all the sea and sky."

But no one of gods | or elves will grant
That we both together should be."
If a worthy hero wields it."
To fare through the wild fells,
Skirnir rode into Jotunheim to Gymir's house. There were fierce dogs bound before the gate of the fence which was around Gerth's hall. He rode to where a herdsman sat on a hill, and said:
Past the hounds of Gymir here?"

To a destined day has mine age been doomed,
And my life's span thereto laid."

From their caves in the east

Ægir, who was also called Gymir, had prepared ale for the gods, after he had got the mighty kettle, as now has been told. To this feast came Othin and Frigg, his wife. Thor came not, as he was on a journey in the East. Sif, Thor's wife, was there, and Brag, with Ithun, his wife. Tyr, who had but one hand, was there; the wolf Fenrir had bitten off his other hand when they had bound him. There were Njorth and Skathi his wife, Freyr and Freyja, and Vithar, the son of Othin. Loki was there, and Freyr's servants Byggvir and Beyla. Many were there of the gods and elves.

Ægir had two serving-men, Fimafeng and Eldir. Glittering gold they had in place of firelight; the ale came in of itself; and great was the peace. The guests praised much the ability of Ægir's serving-men. Loki might not endure that, and he slew Fimafeng. Then the gods shook their shields and howled at Loki and drove him away to the forest, and thereafter set to drinking again. Loki turned back, and outside he met Eldir. Loki spoke to him:
The sons of the glorious gods?"
The sons of the glorious gods;
No friend in words shalt thou find."
For the feast I fain would see;
And their mead with venom I mix."
And fain the feast wouldst see,
Think well lest they wipe it on thee."
If thou speakest too much to me."
Then Loki went into the hall, but when they who were there saw who had entered, they were all silent.
I, Lopt, from a journey long,
Fair mead for a drink to me."
Ye gods, and no answer give?
Or bid me forth to fare."
No more in their midst for thee;
To find at their mighty feasts."
That we both our blood have mixed;
Unless it were brought for us both."
Find a seat at our feast;
Then Vithar arose and poured drink for Loki; but before he drank he spoke to the gods:
ye goddesses, hail! Hail to the holy throng! Save for the god
who yonder sits, Bragi there on the bench."
from my hoard will I give,
And a ring gives Bragi to boot,
So rouse not the great ones to wrath."
Bragi, but both shalt thou lack;
Least brave in battle art thou,
(And shyest thou art of the shot.)"
And pay thee the price of thy lies."
Since chosen as wish-son he was;
of women most lustful in love,
For I wish not that fierce they should fight."
And the dwellers in heaven he hates."
Who led thee to evil life;
And about him thy leg was laid."
The wrath of Gefjun to rouse;
For the fate that is set
The fate of the fight among men;
To the baser, the battle's prize."
To the baser, the battle's prize;
Milking the cows as a maid,
Like witches with charms didst thou work;
Ye should make no speech among men;
Old tales should ne'er be told."
Both in thy bosom have lain."
From the sons of the gods
Till thy fierceness in fight were tried."
Of the ill that now I know;
Thou seest ride home to the hall."
The wrong and shame thou hast wrought;
Though herself she says it not."
Each one as thy lover has lain."
That it sings thee an evil song;
And in grief shalt thou homeward go."
And steeped full sore in sin;
In the arms of thy brother
When Freyja her wind set free."
A lord or a lover or both;
Comes hither, though babes he has borne."
To the gods as a hostage given;
When use did they make of thy mouth."
To the gods as a hostage given;
And foremost of gods is found."
No longer I hold it hid;
Thus hadst thou no worse a hope."
Here in the home of the gods;
And the bound from their fetters he frees."
Thy right hand rent from thee."
And the loss brings longing to both;
In fetters the fall of the gods."
Thy wife once chanced to win;
wast thou paid for the wrong,
Nor wast righted an inch, poor wretch."
Till the gods to destruction go;
if thy tongue is not stilled,
Be fettered, thou forger of ill."
And sold thy sword to boot;
Thou shalt weaponless wait, poor wretch."
And sat in so lofty a seat,
And beat all his body to bits."
Or muttering hard at the mill."
As gods and men do grant;
Their shares of the meat for men;
Hid in straw on the floor,
When heroes were fain to fight."
Why, Loki, leavst thou this not?
No thought of their tongues to take."
Was an evil fate for thee fixed;
As warder of heaven to watch."
On the rocks the gods bind thee
"Though on rocks the gods bind me
When thou badst me come to thy bed;
Shall seek our sins to tell."
Then Sif came forward and poured mead for Loki in a crystal cup, and said:
The crystal cup of old mead;
He took the horn, and drank therefrom:
Who had thee from Hlorrithi's arms,
From his home comes Hlorrithi now;
And deep art thou steeped in sin;
Befouled thou art with thy filth."
Then came Thor forth, and spake:
shall I cleave from thy neck,
And so shall thy life be lost."
Where men shall see thee no more."
To men shouldst thou say no more;
In the thumb of a glove
didst thou hide, thou great one,
And there forgot thou wast Thor."
My right hand shall smite thee
Till all thy bones are broken."
Though thou threatenest thus with thy hammer;
When thy meat thou mightest not get,
And down to the gate of death."
and the sons of the god,
The things that whetted my thoughts;
For thou fightest well, I ween.
Such feasts shalt thou make no more;
(And thy back shall be burnt with fire.)"

And after that Loki hid himself in Franang's waterfall in the guise of a salmon, and there the gods took him. He was bound with the bowels of his son Vali, but his son Narfi was changed to a wolf. Skathi took a poison-snake and fastened it up over Loki's face, and the poison dropped thereon. Sigyn, Loki's wife, sat there and held a shell under the poison, but when the shell was full she bore away the poison, and meanwhile the poison dropped on Loki. Then he struggled so hard that the whole earth shook therewith; and now that is called an earthquake.

TheLayOfThrym.Title: The Lay of Thrym
TheLayOfThrym.Source:
An ancient Icelandic text, edited by Guðni Jónsson.
Translation by Henry Adams Bellows.

linesEN: If thy hammer is brought not
that were set for the women;

There was a king in Sweden named Nithuth. He had two sons and one daughter; her name was Bothvild. There were three brothers, sons of a king of the Finns: one was called Slagfith, another Egil, the third Völund. They went on snowshoes and hunted wild beasts. They came into Ulfdalir and there they built themselves a house; there was a lake there which is called Ulfsjar. Early one morning they found on the shore of the lake three women, who were spinning flax. Near them were their swan garments, for they were Valkyries. Two of them were daughters of King Hlothver, Hlathguth the Swan-White and Hervor the All-Wise, and the third was Olrun, daughter of Kjar from Valland. These did they bring home to their hall with them. Egil took Olrun, and Slagfith Swan-White, and Völund All-Wise. There they dwelt seven winters; but then they flew away to find battles, and came back no more. Then Egil set forth on his snowshoes to follow Olrun, and Slagfith followed Swan White, but Völund stayed in Ulfdalir. He was a most skillful man, as men know from old tales. King Nithuth had him taken by force, as the poem here tells.
On the shore of the sea
at the end of the hall;
from the hills of the Rhine;
from the end of the hall;
King Nithuth gave to his daughter Bothvild the gold ring that he had taken from the bast rope in Völund's house, and he himself wore the sword that Völund had had. The queen spake:

So was it done: the sinews in his knee-joints were cut, and he was set in an island which was near the mainland, and was called Sævarstath. There he smithied for the king all kinds of precious things. No man dared to go to him, save only the king himself. Völund spake:
or the men of the hall,
from the end of the hall;
Though a wife I should have
And a child I should have
There is no man so high
from thy horse to take thee,
Nor at home shall they rob me of rest."
Why so pale round the nose?
By the dead hast thou lain of late?
Thou wast not born for the bride."
My home 'neath the rocks I have;
Let the gods their bond not break."
And I give her alone of the gods."
O'er the bride that shines so bright?
Who was bought with rings to bear thee?"
From winning, thou guest so wise,
That now I wish to know."
Dwarf, of the doom of men:
The Ways is it called by the Wanes;
The Moist by the holy ones high."
Dwarf, of the doom of men:
The Wanes The Weaver of Winds;
Dwarf, of the doom of men.:
The Wheel in the house of hell;
The elves The Teller of Time."
Dwarf, of the doom of men:
The Deceiver of Dvalin the dwarfs;
All-Glowing the sons of the gods."
Dwarf, of the doom of men:
The Wanes call them Kites of the Wind;
The Helmet of Secrets in hell."
Dwarf, of the doom of men:
The Neigher the holy ones high;
Dwarf, of the doom of men:
The Wanes The Hush of the Winds;
The dwarfs The Shelter of Day."
Dwarf, of the doom of men:
The Wave is it called by the Wanes;
For the dwarfs its name is The Deep."
Dwarf, of the doom of men:
In each of all the worlds?"
By the Wanes is it Wildfire called;
The Swift in the house of hell."

Growth in the world of the Wanes;
Dwarf, of the doom of men:
In the world of the Wanes The Foaming;
The day has caught thee, dwarf!
(Now the sun shines here in the hall.)"
I was snowed on with snow,
and a shield lies o'er it;
His hands he shall wash not,
his hair he shall comb not,
be ever proud; For no one of men
shall seek me more Till Loki wanders
loose from his bonds, And to the last strife

They tell in old stories that one of the gods, whose name was Heimdall, went on his way along a certain seashore, and came to a dwelling, where he called himself Rig. According to these stories is the following poem:
on the floor was a fire,
was the best of the dainties.)

GudrunsInciting.Title: Gudrun's Inciting
GudrunsInciting.Source:
An ancient Icelandic text, edited by Guðni Jónsson.
Translation by Henry Adams Bellows.

linesEN: Guthrun went forth to the sea after she had slain Atli. She went out into the sea and fain would drown herself, but she could not sink. The waves bore her across the fjord to the land of King Jonak; he took her as wife; their sons were Sorli and Erp and Hamther. There was brought up Svanhild, Sigurth's daughter; she was married to the mighty Jormunrek. With him was Bikki, who counselled that Randver, the king's son, should have her. This Bikki told to the king. The king had Randver hanged, and Svanhild trodden to death under horses' feet. And when Guthrun learned this, she spake with her sons.
to her sons she bore them;
Of a king of the folk
As the ray of the sun

TheSlayingOfTheNiflungs.Title: The Slaying of The Niflungs
TheSlayingOfTheNiflungs.Source:
An ancient Icelandic text, edited by Guðni Jónsson.
Translation by Henry Adams Bellows.

linesEN: Gunnar and Hogni then took all the gold that Fafnir had had. There was strife between the Gjukungs and Atli, for he held the Gjukungs guilty of Brynhild's death. It was agreed that they should give him Guthrun as wife, and they gave her a draught of forgetfulness to drink before she would consent to be wedded to Atli. The sons of Atli were Erp and Eitil, and Svanhild was the daughter of Sigurth and Guthrun.

King Atli invited Gunnar and Hogni to come to him, and sent as messenger Vingi or Knefröth. Guthrun was aware of treachery, and sent with him a message in runes that they should not come, and as a token she sent to Hogni the ring Andvaranaut and tied a wolf's hair in it.
Gunnar had sought Oddrun, Atli's sister, for his wife, but had her not; then he married Glaumvor, and Hogni's wife was Kostbera; their sons were Solar and Snævar and Gjuki.

And when the Gjukungs came to Atli, then Guthrun be sought her sons to plead for the lives of both the Gjukungs, but they would not do it. Hogni's heart was cut out, and Gunnar was cast into the serpent's den. He smote on the harp and put the serpents to sleep, but an adder stung him in the liver.
